🌏 Shenzhen AI Study Trip

Pre-read for the NUHS-GMIO NCIS delegation · 19-22 July 2026 · Updated 29 Jun per preliminary itinerary
Who
Dennis · Jane · Marissa
When
Sun 19 → Wed 22 Jul
Where
Shenzhen, China
Sponsor
NCIS

📋 Trip at a Glance

"We want to understand how Shenzhen turns AI from demo into production - and what parts can realistically be adapted into Singapore's Epic + Synapxe-governed healthcare environment."

Six stops, six different lenses · Nanshan Hospital and XtalPi added per 29 Jun update

🎮
Tencent
Digital front door · AI agents
☁️
Huawei
Smart hospital infrastructure
🛡️
Ping An
Population health · AI
🦝
XtalPi
AI drug discovery
🧬
Shenzhen Bay Lab
AI + biomedical research
🏥
Nanshan Hospital
Smart hospital · AI

Top 3 things to remember

1
AI at scale needs workflow integration, not separate tools.
2
Data + platform maturity matters more than model hype.
3
Singapore must adapt, not copy - Epic + Synapxe constraints are real.
Songshan Lake Campus · Dongguan

1. Huawei

Songshan Lake Campus, Dongguan · Smart hospital infrastructure & AI compute (~1hr drive from Shenzhen)

🔍 Why it matters

  • Huawei matters less for clinical AI and more for infrastructure AI: cloud, AI compute, networking, cybersecurity, IoT, command centres, smart campus, digital twin.
  • For smart healthcare, the value is in the enterprise backbone: smart hospital infrastructure, connected devices, edge/cloud compute, hospital command centre, smart ward, smart campus, secure architecture.
  • Singapore's AI challenge isn't just 'build a model'. The harder part: where does AI run, how does it connect, how is it secured, who governs it, how does it scale?

🔍 What to observe

AI compute
Cloud / on-prem / edge approach?
Smart hospital
Command centre, bed flow, queue, transport, security, facilities?
Cybersecurity
Identity, access, segmentation, audit logs?
IoT
Sensors, smart ward, asset tracking, environmental?
Digital twin
Simulation of patient flow, ops, campus?

💬 Questions to ask

1For healthcare AI, what should run on cloud, on-prem, or edge?
2How do hospitals design AI infrastructure without creating isolated pilots?
3How do you manage cybersecurity for AI-connected hospital devices?
4What is the architecture behind a smart hospital command centre?
5How do you integrate IoT, EMR, patient flow, and operational dashboards?
6What are the most mature smart hospital use cases already in production?
7How do you measure ROI for smart hospital infrastructure?
8In a tightly governed healthcare system like Singapore, what is the recommended starting point?

🇶🆚 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: Huawei is highly relevant to smart operations rather than direct patient app work.

  • Hospital command centre design
  • AI-enabled operational dashboards
  • Queue, bed, appointment, resource flow prediction
  • AI infrastructure pattern for governed healthcare
  • Edge AI for latency-sensitive / sensitive healthcare use cases
  • Cybersecurity controls for AI platforms
For NUHS, AI cannot be treated as a separate toy sitting outside Epic. We need to understand the enterprise architecture, integration model, auditability, cybersecurity, and governance layer required to support AI safely.
Binhai Mansion · Tencent HQ

2. Tencent

Binhai Mansion, Nanshan District · WeChat + AI agent ecosystem + Tencent Healthcare

🔍 Why it matters

  • Tencent's healthcare power comes from its ecosystem: WeChat, mini-programs, payments, cloud, AI, digital identity, patient engagement, and medical AI tools.
  • Products include WeChat Intelligent Healthcare, Tencent Doctorwork, and AIMIS / Miying - the AI medical imaging & assisted diagnosis platform. AIMIS supports screening for diabetic retinopathy, lung cancer, and esophageal cancer.
  • In March 2026, Tencent launched an AI agent tool integrated with WeChat - direct AI interaction through WeChat's messaging interface.

🔍 What to observe

Digital front door
Can patients book, pay, ask, learn, manage follow-up, and navigate in one app?
AI patient engagement
Triage, FAQ, symptom checking, education, reminders, appointment nav?
AI agent workflow
Cross-service tasks, or just a chatbot?
Data permission
How do patients authorise data/image sharing?
Integration
How tightly connected to hospital systems?

💬 Questions to ask

1How do you decide which healthcare journey is suitable for AI agent automation?
2Is the AI agent connected to real hospital workflows, or mainly patient-facing?
3How do you prevent the AI from giving unsafe medical advice?
4How do clinicians review, override, or audit AI recommendations?
5For imaging AI, how is model performance monitored after deployment?
6How do you manage patient consent when images/reports are shared across institutions?
7What are the biggest barriers when scaling AI across hospitals?
8If a healthcare system already has a major EMR, how would Tencent integrate without disrupting it?

🇶🆚 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: Tencent is highly relevant to NUHS App / MyChart / digital front door thinking. Singapore already has Epic/MyChart + strong governance, so we cannot simply copy WeChat. But we can learn how Tencent designs patient engagement at scale.

  • AI-powered cancer patient navigation
  • Treatment journey reminders and education
  • Cancer care plan digital engagement
  • AI-assisted appointment and follow-up guidance
  • Patient-facing FAQ or concierge integrated with approved content
Singapore already has Epic/MyChart and strong governance, so we cannot simply copy WeChat's model. But we can learn how Tencent designs patient engagement at scale - AI-supported navigation, education, reminders, appointment access, and patient-facing service orchestration.
NSRH
南山区人民医院

3. Nanshan District People's Hospital

Nanshan District, Shenzhen · Smart hospital · AI "clinical teammate" in live use

🔍 Why it matters

  • Probably the most operationally grounded healthcare visit of the trip - if the AI products we heard about from vendors actually work, we should see it here. Nanshan Hospital is a district-level hospital under heavy AI deployment across clinical and operational workflows.
  • The hospital's flagship concept is an "AI teammate with critical thinking" - designed to support, not replace, clinicians. Three core applications: (1) personalized inquiry & diagnosis recommendations, (2) medical record writing, (3) critical care knowledge queries.
  • The leadership framing is "ICU without walls" - extending critical-care expertise to general wards and lower-tier facilities via AI. Party Secretary Hou Tieying has publicly described this as the operating model, not a slogan.
  • 南山区人民医院并不是孤立个案，而是深圳全市医疗 AI 推进的一部分：目前全市医疗机构已落地近 450 个 AI 医疗产品，深圳 970 家社康中心中已有 400 多家部署 AI 辅助诊疗设备。
  • 这是一家区级医院，不是顶尖学术医学中心。这点很关键——我们真正该问的，是 AI 如何在普通医院体系中大规模落地，而不仅仅是在旗舰医院做展示。

🔍 What to observe

AI in real workflow
Is the AI teammate actually used by clinicians on shift? Where does it help vs. annoy?
ICU-without-walls model
How AI extends critical care expertise to lower-tier wards and community sites
Medical record writing
Documentation AI - does it save time, change note quality, raise liability questions?
Diagnosis support
How personalised inquiry & diagnosis recommendations are presented to clinicians
Knowledge queries
How clinicians query critical-care knowledge during live work
Community deployment
How the same AI products extend to 400+ community health centres
Adoption & training
How frontline staff were trained; what they accepted vs. rejected

💬 Questions to ask

1Which of the three "AI teammate" applications delivers the most measurable clinical value today?
2How do you measure whether AI documentation is reducing clinician burnout, or just shifting work?
3What does "critical thinking" mean concretely in the AI teammate - what does it refuse to answer?
4How is the "ICU without walls" model governed when AI recommendations cross facility boundaries?
5How are diagnosis recommendations validated before they reach a clinician?
6What AI failures have you seen in production, and how were they handled?
7How does this hospital integrate AI with HIS/EMR/PACS - embedded or side-by-side?
8For a Singapore hospital under Epic + Synapxe governance, what parts of this model are realistic to adapt?

🇶🆚 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: Nanshan is the proof-of-concept visit. If AI doesn't work in a real Chinese district hospital, the vendor pitches from Tencent/Huawei/Ping An are not yet translating into clinical reality. If it does work, NCIS needs to understand what parts can map onto Epic + Synapxe constraints.

  • AI-assisted documentation (Epic + ambient scribe analogue)
  • AI decision support embedded into clinician workflow, not bolt-on
  • AI extending specialist expertise to general wards (ICU-without-walls concept)
  • Community-hospital extension of the same AI tools
  • Governance model for AI recommendations that influence patient care
  • Liability & accountability when AI assists diagnosis or notes
For NCIS / NUHS, the most honest question at Nanshan is: which of the three "AI teammate" capabilities are mature enough that we could propose a small pilot inside Epic within 12 months, and which are still vendor slideware. We need to leave Shenzhen knowing the difference, not just being impressed by the demo.
Ping An Finance Centre · Futian

4. Ping An

Ping An Finance Centre, Futian District · Insurance + health + AI ecosystem

🔍 Why it matters

  • Ping An connects insurance, health services, online consultation, health management, pharmacies, senior care, and AI into one ecosystem.
  • Ping An Healthcare / Ping An Good Doctor offers online consultations, hospital referrals, appointments, health management, wellness services. The broader ecosystem includes hospital, doctor, pharmacy, and elderly care partnerships.
  • In 2025, Ping An Healthcare announced its AI model and doctor platform had built-in access to DeepSeek's model to support diagnosis and personalised health management.

🔍 What to observe

Health ecosystem
Insurance + care + pharmacy + online consult connection?
Risk prediction
How they identify high-risk patients
Care coordination
AI for follow-up + longitudinal care
Doctor platform
How doctors use AI in real work
Patient engagement
Nudges toward care actions
Financing link
Payment/insurance data supports healthcare planning

💬 Questions to ask

1How do you combine healthcare, insurance, pharmacy, and patient engagement data safely?
2What AI use cases are most mature: risk prediction, triage, care management, claims, doctor assistance?
3How do you validate AI outputs used by doctors?
4How do you avoid over-reliance on AI recommendations?
5How do you measure healthcare outcome improvement, not just platform usage?
6What are the strongest use cases for oncology or chronic disease?
7How do you personalise care without creating privacy or bias concerns?
8How do you manage AI governance across such a large ecosystem?

🇶🆚 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: Ping An is probably one of the most relevant for NCIS oncology population health.

  • Cancer risk stratification
  • Survivorship follow-up
  • Longitudinal care management
  • Patient adherence and digital nudging
  • Predictive analytics for high-risk cancer patients
  • AI-supported care coordination across hospital and community
For NCIS, the most useful learning may not be insurance itself, but Ping An's ability to connect longitudinal patient data, predictive analytics, digital engagement, and care coordination into a scalable operating model.
Guangming Science City · SZBL HQ
SZBL
深圳湾实验室

5. Shenzhen Bay Laboratory (SZBL)

Guangming Science City · AI + biomedical research + translational medicine · Tentative Tue PM (Ping An alternative)

🔍 Why it matters

  • SZBL is relevant to AI + biomedical research + translational medicine.
  • National-level biomedical innovation platform focused on AI, genomics, computational biology, translational medicine, multi-omics integration, computational oncology, and hospital-academia-industry integration.
  • Launched in 2019 with five-year budget of ~US$2 billion from Shenzhen government. Part of Guangming Science City science hub alongside Shenzhen Medical Academy of Research and Translation (SMART).

🔍 What to observe

AI + omics
Genomics, multi-omics, computational biology
Oncology
Computational oncology, tumour biology, precision medicine
Translational model
How research moves into hospitals
Data governance
Research data access + protection
Industry link
Academia + hospital + industry collaboration
Evaluation
Validation before clinical use

💬 Questions to ask

1How do you select biomedical AI projects with real clinical value?
2How is multi-omics data linked to clinical data?
3What are the strongest AI use cases in oncology today?
4How do hospitals participate in translational AI research?
5How do you handle data governance and patient consent?
6How do you move from research model to clinical workflow?
7What validation is required before AI can influence patient care?
8What type of cross-border collaboration is realistic with Singapore/NCIS?

🇶🆚 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: This is highly relevant to NCIS.

  • Computational oncology
  • Cancer risk stratification
  • Genomics + clinical data analytics
  • Translational AI research
  • AI-supported clinical trial matching
  • Longitudinal cancer outcome analytics
  • Research-to-care implementation model
For NCIS, the most important learning is how Shenzhen connects research, hospital data, industry platforms, and implementation pathways - so AI does not remain only in papers but can become useful in real oncology care.
XtPi
晶泰医疗

6. XtalPi (晶泰医疗)

Times Technology Building, Shennan Ave, Shenzhen · AI + quantum physics + robotics for drug discovery

🔍 Why it matters

  • XtalPi is the only visit on the trip focused on AI for pharmaceutical R&D, not directly on hospital operations - but it is the most relevant visit for NCIS oncology pipeline thinking if Singapore wants to participate in AI-driven drug discovery or translational research partnerships.
  • Founded in 2014 by three MIT quantum physicists; headquartered in Shenzhen (Times Technology Building, Shennan Avenue) with a US office in Cambridge, MA. Listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange.
  • Core thesis: combine quantum physics simulation + AI generative models + robotic wet-lab automation into a closed-loop discovery platform. XtalPi says the platform can compress drug discovery timelines from ~4 years to ~2 years.
  • 客户基础方面，晶泰已与全球约 80% 的头部制药公司合作。近期案例包括与 Signet Therapeutics 的合作，在约 6 个月内识别出胃癌新靶点的临床前候选分子，并于 2025 年扩展到新的 first-in-class 癌症项目。
  • 对 NCIS 来说，这次行程中最现实的跨境合作机会，很可能不是医院对医院，而是与晶泰这样的研发平台合作——新加坡 / NCIS 提供临床数据与试验资源，深圳提供 AI、化学与自动化能力。

🔍 What to observe

Closed-loop workflow
How AI dry-lab predictions feed robotic wet-lab experiments and back
Quantum + AI
Where quantum physics actually helps vs. classical ML would be enough
Oncology pipeline
Current oncology targets and stage of preclinical / clinical work
Big-pharma model
How XtalPi partners with global pharma - what they keep, what they share
Automation
What the autonomous lab actually does end-to-end
Data & IP
Who owns generated molecules, data, and IP

💬 Questions to ask

1For an oncology target, how do you decide AI generation vs. traditional medicinal chemistry?
2How do you validate AI-generated molecules before they enter animal studies?
3Where does quantum physics actually outperform classical AI in your pipeline?
4What is the realistic timeline from AI-generated molecule to IND filing today?
5For a hospital partner (NCIS), what is the lowest-friction collaboration model?
6How do you handle clinical data linkage if XtalPi partners with hospital systems?
7What IP and data-sharing terms are realistic for a Singapore partner?
8What does XtalPi NOT do well today, where classical pharma CROs still win?

🇶🆚 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: NCIS will not run an AI drug discovery platform, but Singapore could plausibly partner with one - and XtalPi is the most credible Shenzhen candidate. The questions we ask here determine whether a future MoU is realistic.

  • AI-driven preclinical candidate generation for NCIS-relevant oncology targets
  • Singapore clinical-trial partnership model (NCIS provides patient access, XtalPi provides molecules)
  • Cross-border IP & data governance for hospital-pharma R&D
  • AI validation framework before any human exposure
  • Realistic timeline from AI-generated molecule to first-in-human study
For NCIS, the value of the XtalPi visit is not to copy the platform but to map the partnership model - what would a realistic Singapore-Shenzhen oncology AI R&D collaboration actually look like, what data/IP terms are required, and which 1-2 NCIS-relevant targets could be a credible first pilot. If XtalPi is interested in Singapore clinical access, GMIO/NCIS should be ready with a concrete proposal within 30 days of return.

📌 Must-Ask Questions Across All Visits

Keep these in your notebook and reuse at every site.

A. Scaling beyond pilot

  1. What was the first AI use case that successfully scaled?
  2. What made it scale: technology, leadership, workflow, funding, or governance?
  3. How many AI pilots did not scale, and what were the common reasons?

B. Integration

  1. Is AI embedded inside the core clinical system, or a separate platform?
  2. How do you integrate AI with EMR / HIS / PACS / LIS / CRM / appointment systems?
  3. How do you avoid creating another silo?

C. Governance

  1. Who approves clinical AI use?
  2. How do you validate accuracy before go-live?
  3. How do you monitor performance after go-live?
  4. What happens when AI gives a wrong recommendation?

D. Adoption

  1. How do you convince clinicians to trust AI?
  2. Is AI optional, recommended, or mandatory in workflow?
  3. How do you manage alert fatigue or over-reliance?

E. Data

  1. What data is needed for AI to work well?
  2. How do you handle incomplete, inconsistent, or unstructured data?
  3. How do you handle patient consent and data sharing?

F. Singapore applicability

  1. If a healthcare system is bound by a major EMR vendor contract and central IT governance, what AI implementation model would you recommend?
  2. What can be done safely as a first step without disrupting enterprise systems?
  3. What should not be attempted too early?
  4. What capability must be built internally, instead of depending fully on vendors?

🎤 Your Personal Positioning

When introducing yourself, a strong framing line:

"I am from NUHS GMIO, supporting outpatient, ambulatory, MyChart/NUHS App, and Epic-related digital transformation work. I am currently very interested in how AI can be safely embedded into healthcare workflows, especially within enterprise EMR and governed IT environments. For this trip, I am hoping to learn how Shenzhen organisations move AI from idea to scalable production use cases, and what lessons can be adapted to Singapore's Epic and Synapxe-governed environment."

📝 What to Pay Attention To During the Trip

Don't just record "what AI they have" - record these five things instead:

What to captureWhy it matters
Use caseWhat problem does the AI solve?
WorkflowWhere exactly does it sit in the user journey?
IntegrationWhat system / data does it connect to?
GovernanceWho approves, monitors, and owns it?
OutcomeWhat measurable benefit was achieved?

📊 Suggested Post-Trip Report Structure

  1. Executive Summary - key learnings, what's relevant to NCIS/NUHS, what's not directly transferable
  2. Observations by Organisation - Huawei, Tencent, Nanshan District People's Hospital, Ping An, SZBL, XtalPi
  3. Key Themes - AI at scale needs workflow integration; data/platform maturity beats model hype; governance must be designed early; AI must solve operational pain, not just impress leadership; Singapore must adapt, not copy
  4. Singapore / NCIS Applicability - what can be done in Epic/MyChart, what needs Synapxe governance, what can be sandboxed, what needs enterprise architecture approval
  5. Recommended Follow-Up Actions - strengthen GMIO GROSS / Epic PCR AI Agent, identify 2-3 NCIS oncology AI use cases, explore AI patient navigation + smart ops dashboard, start governance discussion with Synapxe early

🌟 Top 5 Takeaways to Aim For

  1. How Shenzhen chooses AI use cases that are worth scaling
  2. How AI is embedded into workflow instead of sitting outside the system
  3. How they govern AI safety, cybersecurity, privacy, and clinical accountability
  4. How they measure outcome and ROI after go-live
  5. How Singapore can adapt the model within Epic contract and Synapxe governance constraints
